factors.summary: Management has lowered its forward guidance for revenue and earnings due to the impact of the pandemic.

evidence.text: We expect revenue to be down 10% to 15% from last year versus our initial guidance for growth of 4% to 8% (and down from our first quarter expectations of a 11% to 17% decline). We now expect Diluted EPS from continuing operations in the range of $7.31 to $8.11 for the year (as compared to our first quarter expected range of $7.07 to $8.07).
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Management has significantly lowered revenue and earnings guidance due to the pandemic.

factors.summary: The company's long-term strategic direction is clear, focusing on market leadership and operational efficiency despite current challenges.

evidence.text: Our quarterly dividend plans are unchanged, most recently $0.77 per share, or more than $115 million in total payments for the year. We repurchased $100 million of stock in the first quarter of the $400 million we had planned to repurchase for the year. However, we placed the remaining repurchase plans for 2020 on hold and we will review plans for the third and fourth quarters as the year progresses.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: While the company maintains its strategic direction, there is no indication of significant changes or improvements.

factors.summary: The company has implemented cost reduction programs and is managing through the pandemic, which are acting as catalysts to improve margins and manage expenses.

evidence.text: In April 2020, we implemented cost reduction actions to realize $115 million of SG&A savings for the balance of the year. In connection with these cost saving actions, we incurred pre-tax charges of $10 million in the second quarter of 2020 related primarily to personnel severance and benefits and facility exit costs. Also, during the second quarter of 2020, we incurred $3 million of expenses for facility cleaning and supplies to ensure the health and safety of our employees.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: The company has implemented cost reduction programs to improve margins and manage expenses.

factors.summary: Capital expenditures are decreasing compared to prior periods.

evidence.text: Capital expenditures were $44 million in the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to $54 million in the same period of 2019.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Capital Resources

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Capital expenditures have decreased compared to prior periods, indicating a possible contraction in investment activities.

factors.summary: Debt leverage is stable with a slight increase in the debt-to-total-capital ratio.

evidence.text: Our debt-to-total-capital ratio increased to 119.7% at June 30, 2020 from 117.0% at December 31, 2019.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Capital Resources

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: There is a slight increase in debt leverage, but it is not significant enough to warrant a strong sentiment label.

factors.summary: Shareholder returns are being managed with reduced buybacks and maintained dividends.

evidence.text: We repurchased $100 million of shares for the six months ended June 30, 2020 and returned $59 million to shareholders through dividend payments.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Capital Resources

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The company is managing shareholder returns through reduced buybacks and maintained dividends, which is a mixed approach.

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation is improving due to lower working capital requirements.

evidence.text: We generated cash from operating activities during the six months ended June 30, 2020 primarily due to lower working capital requirements resulting from the business downturn caused by the COVID-19 pandemic.
evidence.subsection: Liquidity and Capital Resources

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Free cash flow generation is improving due to lower working capital requirements.

factors.summary: The divestiture of Kysor Warren has been completed, impacting the business.

evidence.text: There were no losses related to this sale for the six months ended June 30, 2020. Refer to Note 6 in the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements for additional information on this divestiture.

sentiment.rationale: The completion of the divestiture of Kysor Warren is an operational change with no clear positive or negative sentiment.

factors.summary: Economic conditions are a headwind due to the impact of COVID-19 and related business downturn.

evidence.text: The current and uncertain future impact of the COVID-19 outbreak, including its effect on the demand for our products, our customers and our supply chain, is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.
evidence.subsection: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted our business operations and results of operations and is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.

sentiment.rationale: Economic conditions are challenging due to the impact of COVID-19, acting as a headwind.

factors.summary: Interest rates are beneficial with lower borrowing costs.

evidence.text: Interest expense, net of $16 million in the six months ended June 30, 2020 decreased $8 million from $24 million in the six months ended June 30, 2019 primarily due to lower borrowing costs and lower borrowings.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Consolidated Results

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Lower interest rates are leading to decreased borrowing costs, which is beneficial.

factors.summary: Foreign exchange movements are having a negative impact with unfavorable rates affecting margins.

evidence.text: Net sales decreased 30% in the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019. Sales volumes decreased 18%, the loss of sales from our divested Kysor Warren business contributed 12%, and unfavorable foreign currency exchange rates contributed 1%, which was partially offset by 1% of favorable combined price and mix.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Results by Segment

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Foreign exchange movements are causing significant negative impacts on margins.

factors.summary: The company has demonstrated some ability to mitigate the impact of commodity price volatility through price increases and other measures, but has also faced pricing pressures leading to decreased gross profit margins.

evidence.text: We seek to mitigate the impact of volatility in commodity prices through a combination of price increases, commodity contracts, improved production efficiency, and cost reduction initiatives. We also partially mitigate volatility in the prices of these commodities by entering into futures contracts and fixed forward contracts.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: Gross profit margins for the six months ended June 30, 2020 decreased 170 bps to 26.5% compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019. Our profit margin decreased 200 bps from unfavorable combined price and mix, 120 bps from factory inefficiencies, and 90 bps from higher product warranties and other product costs.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Consolidated Results

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: While the company can mitigate some volatility, pricing pressures have led to decreased gross margins.

factors.summary: The revenue mix has shifted negatively due to lower-margin segments and unfavorable factors such as the pandemic, lower sales volumes, and foreign currency exchange rates.

evidence.text: Net sales decreased $158 million to $941 million in the second quarter of 2020 driven by volume declines in each of our operating segments. The Residential Heating & Cooling segment saw a 6% decrease in net sales, the Commercial Heating & Cooling segment had a 28% decrease, and the Refrigeration segment had a 27% decline in net sales.
evidence.subsection: Financial Overview

evidence.text: Net sales decreased 30% in the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019. Sales volumes decreased 18%, the loss of sales from our divested Kysor Warren business contributed 12%, and unfavorable foreign currency exchange rates contributed 1%, which was partially offset by 1% of favorable combined price and mix.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Results by Segment

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: The revenue mix has shifted negatively due to lower-margin segments and unfavorable factors.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is decelerating due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and lower sales volumes, leading to a significant decline in net sales.

evidence.text: Our revised downward financial outlook remains but we made slight adjustments to our expectations. Currently, we continue to believe that the North America unitary HVAC and refrigeration market will be negatively impacted about 20% this year by the pandemic. Since the first quarter of 2020, we have reset our financial expectations for the year based on that level of market impact and now expect revenue to be down 10% to 15% from last year versus our initial guidance for growth of 4% to 8%
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: Net sales decreased 12% in the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019 due to a 11% decline in sales volumes and a 2% decline related to the sale of our Kysor Warren business in the first quarter of 2019, partially offset by 1% from favorable combined price and mix.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Consolidated Results

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Organic growth is decelerating significantly due to the pandemic and lower sales.

factors.summary: Demand trends are negative, as evidenced by a deceleration in overall demand and a significant decrease in sales volumes due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted our business operations and caused a significant unfavorable impact on our results of operations. In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, various national, state, and local governments issued decrees prohibiting certain businesses from continuing to operate and certain classes of workers from reporting to work. These decrees have resulted in supply chain disruption and higher employee absenteeism in our factories. Additionally, certain of our manufacturing facilities experienced short-term suspensions of operations for COVID-19 employee health concerns during the second quarter of 2020.
evidence.subsection: Second Quarter of 2020 Compared to Second Quarter of 2019 - Consolidated Results

evidence.text: Net sales decreased 12% in the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019 due to a 11% decline in sales volumes and a 2% decline related to the sale of our Kysor Warren business in the first quarter of 2019, partially offset by 1% from favorable combined price and mix.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Consolidated Results

sentiment.label: very_negative

evidence.text: Net sales decreased 6% in the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019. Sales volumes declined 7% partially offset by favorable combined price and mix of 1%.

factors.summary: The end markets are experiencing a slowdown, particularly affecting the Commercial Heating & Cooling and Refrigeration segments more severely than the Residential Heating & Cooling segment. The overall momentum is negative due to decreased net sales across these segments, with the Commercial segment seeing the largest decline.

factors.summary: Labor costs are being managed effectively, with notable reductions achieved through cost-saving measures such as employee terminations and facility closures, as well as declines in salaries and incentive compensation.

evidence.text: In April 2020, we implemented cost reduction actions to realize $115 million of SG&A savings for the balance of the year. In connection with these cost saving actions, we incurred pre-tax charges of $10 million in the second quarter of 2020 related primarily to personnel severance and benefits and facility exit costs.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: SG&A declined $37 million to $261 million for the six months ended June 30, 2020 compared to $298 million for the six months ended June 30, 2019 primarily due to lower salaries and incentive compensation costs, the sale of our Kysor Warren business in the first quarter of 2019, and lower discretionary expenditures.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Consolidated Results

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Labor costs have been effectively managed with notable reductions.

factors.summary: Gross and operating margins are under pressure, experiencing compression due to multiple factors including lower sales volumes, unfavorable price and mix, factory inefficiencies, and elevated product warranty costs.

evidence.text: Gross profit margin in the second quarter of 2020 decreased 90 basis points ('bps') to 29.3% compared to 30.2% in the second quarter of 2019. We saw margin decreases of 200 bps from lower combined price and mix, 100 bps from factory inefficiencies, and 50 bps from higher product warranty costs.

evidence.text: Gross profit margins for the six months ended June 30, 2020 decreased 170 bps to 26.5% compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019. factors.summary: The supply chain is negatively impacted due to ongoing disruptions from the COVID-19 pandemic, including extended lead times, supply chain disruption, and factory closures. The situation remains uncertain with potential for further disruptions.

evidence.text: In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, various national, state, and local governments issued decrees prohibiting certain businesses from continuing to operate and certain classes of workers from reporting to work. These decrees have resulted in supply chain disruption and higher employee absenteeism in our factories. Additionally, certain of our manufacturing facilities experienced short-term suspensions of operations for COVID-19 employee health concerns during the second quarter of 2020.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: Those decrees have resulted in supply chain disruption and higher absenteeism in our factories. It remains unclear how long these decrees will remain in place, what additional decrees may be instituted, and the impact they may have on our company.
evidence.subsection: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted our business operations and results of operations and is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.

sentiment.label: very_negative
sentiment.rationale: Supply chain is disrupted due to COVID-19, leading to significant operational issues.

factors.summary: The company has implemented cost reduction actions, such as restructuring and employee terminations, which have delivered measurable savings. These actions include SG&A savings of $115 million for the balance of 2020 and incurred pre-tax charges of $10 million in the second quarter related to personnel severance and benefits as well as facility exit costs. Additionally, restructuring charges of approximately $11 million were incurred in the first half of 2020 due to COVID-19's impact, including selective facility closures and sales and marketing activity cancellations.

evidence.text: In April 2020, we implemented cost reduction actions to realize $115 million of SG&A savings for the balance of the year. In connection with these cost saving actions, we incurred pre-tax charges of $10 million in the second quarter of 2020 related primarily to personnel severance and benefits and facility exit costs.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: Restructuring charges were approximately $11 million in the six months ended June 30, 2020 and related to several cost reduction actions taken in response to the economic impact of COVID-19 on our business. These actions consisted of employee terminations for positions that are no longer needed to support the business, selective facility closures, and cancellations of certain sales and marketing activities.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Consolidated Results

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Cost reduction actions have delivered measurable savings through restructuring and employee terminations.

factors.summary: Management is exhibiting caution due to the ongoing effects of the COVID-19 pandemic and associated challenges, such as supply chain disruptions, employee absenteeism, manufacturing facility suspensions, and increased health and safety costs. Additionally, restructuring actions and uncertain market conditions contribute to this cautious stance.

evidence.text: The magnitude of the impact of COVID-19 remains unpredictable and we, therefore, continue to anticipate potential supply chain disruptions, employee absenteeism, and short-term suspensions of manufacturing facilities, and additional health and safety costs related to the COVID-19 pandemic that could unfavorably impact our business. We also anticipate significant challenges in the remainder of 2020 due to uncertain market conditions.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: We believe that the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic could have a material adverse effect on the results of our operations, financial position and cash flows as of and for the year ended December 31, 2020.
evidence.subsection: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted our business operations and results of operations and is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Management is cautious due to the ongoing effects of the COVID-19 pandemic and associated challenges.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is decreasing due to lower demand, lower sales volumes, factory inefficiencies, and restructuring actions.

evidence.text: Gross profit margin in the second quarter of 2020 decreased 90 basis points ('bps') to 29.3% compared to 30.2% in the second quarter of 2019. We saw margin decreases of 200 bps from lower combined price and mix, 100 bps from factory inefficiencies, and 50 bps from higher product warranty costs.
evidence.subsection: Second Quarter of 2020 Compared to Second Quarter of 2019 - Consolidated Results

evidence.text: Segment profit for the six months ended June 30, 2020 decreased $80 million compared to the six months ended June 30, 2019 due to $58 million of non-recurring insurance proceeds for lost profits related to the Marshalltown tornado, $33 million from lower sales volumes, $17 million from lower factory absorption and efficiency, $15 million from non-recurring warranty adjustments and other product costs, $11 million from unfavorable mix, and $1 million from lower income from equity method investments.
evidence.subsection: Year-to-Date through June 30, 2020 Compared to Year-to-Date through June 30, 2019 - Results by Segment

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Capacity utilization is decreasing due to lower demand and factory inefficiencies.

factors.summary: The company exhibits significant sensitivity to various factors, including economic conditions, commodity price volatility, and supply chain disruptions. These sensitivities can have both negative and mixed impacts on the company's operations and financial performance.

evidence.text: The principal raw materials used in our manufacturing processes are steel, copper, and aluminum. In recent years, pricing volatility for these commodities and related components, including the impact of imposed tariffs on the import of certain of our raw materials and components, has impacted us and the HVACR industry in general. We seek to mitigate the impact of volatility in commodity prices through a combination of price increases, commodity contracts, improved production efficiency, and cost reduction initiatives. We also partially mitigate volatility in the prices of these commodities by entering into futures contracts and fixed forward contracts.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: The overall economic environment is a headwind (expansion, strong GDP) or slowing (slowdown, recession risk) for the company's business.
evidence.subsection: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted our business operations and results of operations and is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: The company faces significant sensitivity to economic conditions and supply chain disruptions, posing risks.

factors.summary: The near-term risks are predominantly negative, with significant disruptions and increased costs due to the COVID-19 pandemic, including supply chain issues, employee absenteeism, health and safety expenses, and challenges in market conditions. Additionally, the ongoing impact of the pandemic on demand, customers, and the supply chain is expected to continue affecting the company's operations, financial position, and cash flows.

evidence.text: The magnitude of the impact of COVID-19 remains unpredictable and we, therefore, continue to anticipate potential supply chain disruptions, employee absenteeism and short-term suspensions of manufacturing facilities, and additional health and safety costs related to the COVID-19 pandemic that could unfavorably impact our business. We also anticipate significant challenges in the remainder of 2020 due to uncertain market conditions.
evidence.subsection: Business Overview

evidence.text: The current and uncertain future impact of the COVID-19 outbreak, including its effect on the demand for our products, our customers and our supply chain, is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.
evidence.subsection: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted our business operations and results of operations and is expected to continue to impact our results of operations, financial position and cash flows.
